driver.body: Owning cheap electrons is necessary but not sufficient. The first driver secured a low **average** cost of power; it does nothing for the cost that actually shows up in the unit economics — the **marginal** cost at the instant a vehicle plugs in, a courier tops up, or a retail customer draws a session [S-025]. The second driver is the operational engine that closes that gap: the machinery that decides **when to buy, when to charge, and what to price**, so a cheap average landed cost becomes a cheap marginal cost at the moment of each interaction [S-025]. This is where the domain's distinctive formula-and-code palette lives — the load-reading, price-setting, dispatch-solving logic that the two mechanisms below make concrete.

mechanisms.body:
Demand is not flat, and **the shape of the curve is the whole game** [S-026]. Two patterns dominate, and both are read off the network-average kW drawn, normalised to peak = 100.

The daily curve has two peaks. A sharp morning ramp runs 06:00–09:00, driven by fleet pre-positioning plus commuter charging. A taller, longer evening peak runs 17:00–21:00, stacking fleet recharge, courier surge, and retail on top of one another. The trough sits at 00:00–05:00 — the hours when landed cost is lowest and demand is thinnest at the same time [S-026]. The economic opportunity is precisely **the gap between the two**: expensive power meets peak demand in the evening, cheap power meets no demand overnight.

The seasonal curve, indexed to Jan = 100, has two ridges that make the problem worse. A cooling-driven summer peak (Jun–Aug) lands in the tropical metros, where grid prices spike exactly when air-conditioning load does. A Q4 logistics ridge is driven by D3's delivery surge [S-204]. Neither ridge coincides with a cheap-power window — which is the entire reason **dispatch, not just procurement, is where margin is made or lost**. Reading the curve chains directly into acting on it [S-026].

Given the curve, the engine does two coupled things off **one margin object**: it prices demand to flatten the peaks, and it dispatches charging and stored energy into the troughs [S-027].

The pricing side sets a per-session price as a base landed cost plus a **congestion term** that rises with local utilisation and a **scarcity term** that rises with the live grid price — then floors the result to protect customer trust and caps it to protect trust the other way. In the formula, util(s,t) ∈ [0,1] is site utilisation, grid(t) is the live wholesale price, and β and γ are tuned per city [S-027]. The dispatch side solves, over a rolling 24-hour horizon, when to draw from storage versus the grid to minimise landed cost subject to meeting the forecast load [S-027].

The engine pays. Live, peak-to-trough load has flattened by **18 points of the normalised index** versus a flat-tariff baseline, and storage arbitrage captured **$0.019/kWh of additional margin** on the 22% of volume it touches [S-205]. Crucially, the same engine is what lets Kepler hand D1 and D3 a stable, low internal transfer price: having flattened its own exposure, it can offer the fleet a fixed **$0.094/kWh** and D3 **$0.101/kWh** — both far below the **$0.163 retail benchmark**, and that gap is the marginal-cost reduction the flywheel promised [S-206]. Dispatch stability chains into the third driver: a network that is economically stable is the precondition for being reliable, and **reliability is where trust is minted** [S-027].
